original_abstract: Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) is a cornerstone of modern metabolomics, enabling the rapid quantitation of cellular components based on accurate mass and chromatographic retention time. Driven by the need for high-throughput screening and the availability of high-resolution mass spectrometers, there has been a significant shift toward methods with minimal or even no chromatographic separation. However, eliminating the liquid chromatography dimension poses a substantial risk for misannotating structurally related metabolites in complex cellular extracts. In this study, we demonstrate that even electrospray ionization—despite being considered a "soft" ionization technique—induces in-source fragmentation that yields byproducts identical in mass to other common metabolites. Notable examples include the conversion of nucleotide triphosphates into diphosphates and hexose phosphates into triose phosphates. By evaluating yeast intracellular extract, we identified over 20 instances where in-source fragments could be mistaken for endogenous metabolites. Our findings underscore that robust chromatographic separation remains essential for the accurate identification and quantitation of a broad range of cellular metabolites.
humanized_abstract: Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) is widely considered to be the central instrumentation in metabolomics studies. Because metabolites can be easily quantified based on their accurate mass and known retention time, it is often the method of choice due to the high analytical speed it affords together with the fact that almost all modern mass spectrometers are high resolution (HR). To increase productivity and to fully utilize the power of the modern MS systems, methods that obviate the use of liquid chromatography (LC) or that minimize the number of chromatographic separations required have been frequently developed. However, by removing one of the chromatographic dimensions, the analyses are dramatically increased at risk of misidentifying other species in the complex mix of metabolites found within a cellular extract, the chromatography being partly for that purpose. Here we illustrate the fact that in spite of electrospray ionization being described as a “soft” ionization technique, significant levels of in-source fragmentation products are produced and their exact masses are indistinguishable from those of biologically relevant metabolites (such as nucleotide triphosphates and their thiosephosphate derivatives which are readily ionized into nucleotide diphosphates and 2-carbon fragments—hexose phosphates that are ionized into hexose plus a 3 carbon fragment, etc.). We also provide the examples of yeast intracellular metabolites corresponding to over 20 cases where fragments ionized with the same intensity as the metabolites they derived from. Consequently, their chromatographic separation should be always the warranty of their correct identification and quantification in the metabolome studies.
